contentBlocks.title: Why convert YAML to JSON?
contentBlocks.html:
YAML is human-readable and widely used for configuration files (Docker Compose, Kubernetes, GitHub Actions, Ansible). However, APIs, programming languages, and most tools process JSON natively. Converting YAML to JSON is essential when you need to submit configuration data to an API, validate structure programmatically, or use the data in JavaScript/Python code.

JSON is strictly typed (strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays, objects) and does not depend on indentation — making it less error-prone for automated processing. Converting YAML to JSON also helps validate YAML syntax: if the conversion succeeds, your YAML is valid.

The converter handles all YAML features: nested objects, arrays, multiline strings, anchors, and aliases. Output is properly formatted JSON with 2-space indentation.

All processing happens locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server. No registration, no limits, no tracking.

contentBlocks.title: How does YAML to JSON conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) uses indentation and minimal syntax to represent structured data, while JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) uses curly braces, square brackets, and explicit quotation marks. The converter parses the YAML structure and outputs equivalent JSON with proper formatting.

YAML indentation becomes JSON nesting, YAML dash-prefixed items become JSON array elements, and YAML key-value pairs become JSON object properties. Data types are preserved: strings, numbers, booleans, null values, arrays, and objects all map directly between the two formats.

Converting YAML to JSON is useful when integrating configuration data into web applications, REST APIs, or any system that expects JSON input. The conversion runs entirely in your browser — no data is sent to any server.

contentBlocks.title: Tips for converting YAML to JSON
contentBlocks.description: A few tips to help you avoid common issues during conversion:

items.title: Indentation consistency
items.description: YAML is sensitive to indentation. If your YAML file mixes tabs and spaces, parsing may fail. The converter will report any indentation errors it encounters.

items.title: YAML comments are removed
items.description: JSON does not support comments. Any # comments in your YAML file will not appear in the JSON output. Keep your original YAML if you need to preserve comments.

items.title: Multi-document YAML
items.description: YAML files can contain multiple documents separated by ---. The converter processes the first document by default. Split multi-document files before conversion if needed.

items.title: Anchors and aliases
items.description: YAML anchors (&) and aliases (*) are resolved during conversion. The JSON output contains the expanded values, not the YAML references.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about YAML to JSON conversion

items.question: Is converting YAML to JSON free?
items.answer: Yes, this converter is completely free with no limits. No registration, no watermarks.

items.question: Does the converter upload my files?
items.answer: No. All processing happens locally in your browser. Your files never leave your device.

items.question: Can I convert multiple files at once?
items.answer: Yes, the converter supports batch conversion. Add multiple files and convert them all simultaneously.

items.question: Does the converter work on mobile?
items.answer: Yes, the converter works on any device with a modern web browser, including smartphones and tablets.

items.question: Is converting YAML to JSON safe?
items.answer: Yes. The entire conversion runs locally in your browser. Your data is never sent to any server. When you close the page, all data is automatically cleared from memory.

items.question: Will YAML comments be preserved?
items.answer: No. JSON does not support comments, so any # comments in your YAML file are discarded during conversion. Keep your original YAML file if you need to preserve comments.

items.question: Is the conversion lossless?
items.answer: Yes for data values. All strings, numbers, booleans, null values, arrays, and objects are preserved exactly. Only YAML-specific features like comments and anchors are not carried over to JSON.
